query: javascript memory leaks devtools workflow interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this JavaScript prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: MDN Memory management
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for reachability, garbage collection basics, and what keeps JavaScript objects alive.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on memory-management fundamentals and object reachability.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on browser-specific leak sources like detached DOM nodes, forgotten listeners, timers, and heap-snapshot workflow.

realWorldUseCases: In long-lived frontend apps, leaks show up as rising memory, slower interactions, and eventual tab crashes.
actionableExamples: A deterministic dispose() path is one of the most reliable anti-leak patterns for UI components and utilities.
followUpConfusion: The garbage collector only frees unreachable objects, so leaks are usually retention bugs, not GC bugs.

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers the fundamentals correctly, but our page is stronger on the browser-app incidents engineers actually debug: listeners, timers, detached DOM, and cache growth.
actionableExamples: The canonical guide still explains the memory model clearly, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is sharper on the likely follow-up confusion: GC is not failing, the object is still reachable through some retention path.

actionableExamples: Add one short retainers-path example later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical memory-management reference, but our page is stronger for this prompt because it turns reachability into a concrete browser leak-debugging workflow.

competitors.label: Chrome DevTools memory problems
competitors.selectionReason: Chrome DevTools documentation is the strongest practical reference for heap snapshots, allocation tools, and investigating memory growth in browser apps.
theirStrengths: It is deeper on heap snapshots, allocation tools, and DevTools workflow than a compact interview answer can be.
theirGaps: It is more tool-focused and less compact on the conceptual rule that leaks are retention bugs caused by live reference paths.

realWorldUseCases: event listeners, timers, detached DOM nodes, unbounded caches
followUpConfusion: Think in terms of graph reachability: roots -> references -> objects.

realWorldUseCases: The DevTools docs are practical, but our page is more explicit about the common leak sources teams should suspect before opening tooling.
actionableExamples: The official DevTools guide is deeper on the mechanics of heap snapshots, allocation instrumentation, and memory debugging workflow, so it wins on example depth.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the likely interview follow-up: leaks are usually retention paths, not a broken garbage collector.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit retained detached-node example if we later want to compete more directly with the DevTools guide.

competitors.winReason: The DevTools guide is deeper on tooling, but our page is stronger for the interview prompt because it combines leak sources, mental model, and workflow into one concise answer.
